1.play: Enter the ARCHBISHOP OF CANTERBURY, and the BISHOP OF ELY

2.play: CANTERBURY: My lord, I'll tell you; that self bill is urged,

3.play: Which in the eleventh year of the last king's reign

4.play: Was like, and had indeed against us pass'd,

5.play: But that the scambling and unquiet time

6.play: Did push it out of farther question.

7.play: ELY: But how, my lord, shall we resist it now?

8.play: CANTERBURY: It must be thought on. If it pass against us,

9.play: We lose the better half of our possession:

10.play: For all the temporal lands which men devout

11.play: By testament have given to the church

12.play: Would they strip from us; being valued thus:

13.play: As much as would maintain, to the king's honour,

14.play: Full fifteen earls and fifteen hundred knights,

15.play: Six thousand and two hundred good esquires;

16.play: And, to relief of lazars and weak age,

17.play: Of indigent faint souls past corporal toil.

18.play: A hundred almshouses right well supplied;

19.play: And to the coffers of the king beside,

20.play: A thousand pounds by the year: thus runs the bill.

21.play: ELY: This would drink deep.

22.play: CANTERBURY: 'Twould drink the cup and all.

23.play: ELY: But what prevention?

24.play: CANTERBURY: The king is full of grace and fair regard.

25.play: ELY: And a true lover of the holy church.

26.play: CANTERBURY: The courses of his youth promised it not.

27.play: The breath no sooner left his father's body,

28.play: But that his wildness, mortified in him,

29.play: Seem'd to die too; yea, at that very moment

30.play: Consideration, like an angel, came

31.play: And whipp'd the offending Adam out of him,

32.play: Leaving his body as a paradise,

33.play: To envelop and contain celestial spirits.

34.play: Never was such a sudden scholar made;

35.play: Never came reformation in a flood,

36.play: With such a heady currance, scouring faults

38.play: So soon did lose his seat and all at once

40.play: ELY: We are blessed in the change.

41.play: CANTERBURY: Hear him but reason in divinity,

42.play: And all-admiring with an inward wish

43.play: You would desire the king were made a prelate:

44.play: Hear him debate of commonwealth affairs,

45.play: You would say it hath been all in all his study:

46.play: List his discourse of war, and you shall hear

47.play: A fearful battle render'd you in music:

48.play: Turn him to any cause of policy,

49.play: The Gordian knot of it he will unloose,

50.play: Familiar as his garter: that, when he speaks,

51.play: The air, a charter'd libertine, is still,

52.play: And the mute wonder lurketh in men's ears,

53.play: To steal his sweet and honey'd sentences;

54.play: So that the art and practic part of life

55.play: Must be the mistress to this theoric:

56.play: Which is a wonder how his grace should glean it,

57.play: Since his addiction was to courses vain,

58.play: His companies unletter'd, rude and shallow,

59.play: His hours fill'd up with riots, banquets, sports,

60.play: And never noted in him any study,

63.play: ELY: The strawberry grows underneath the nettle

64.play: And wholesome berries thrive and ripen best

65.play: Neighbour'd by fruit of baser quality:

66.play: And so the prince obscured his contemplation

67.play: Under the veil of wildness; which, no doubt,

68.play: Grew like the summer grass, fastest by night,

69.play: Unseen, yet crescive in his faculty.

70.play: CANTERBURY: It must be so; for miracles are ceased;

71.play: And therefore we must needs admit the means

74.play: How now for mitigation of this bill

75.play: Urged by the commons? Doth his majesty

78.play: Or rather swaying more upon our part

79.play: Than cherishing the exhibiters against us;

80.play: For I have made an offer to his majesty,

82.play: And in regard of causes now in hand,

83.play: Which I have open'd to his grace at large,

84.play: As touching France, to give a greater sum

85.play: Than ever at one time the clergy yet

86.play: Did to his predecessors part withal.

87.play: ELY: How did this offer seem received, my lord?

88.play: CANTERBURY: With good acceptance of his majesty;

89.play: Save that there was not time enough to hear,

90.play: As I perceived his grace would fain have done,

92.play: Of his true titles to some certain dukedoms

93.play: And generally to the crown and seat of France

95.play: ELY: What was the impediment that broke this off?

96.play: CANTERBURY: The French ambassador upon that instant

97.play: Craved audience; and the hour, I think, is come

98.play: To give him hearing: is it four o'clock?

100.play: CANTERBURY: Then go we in, to know his embassy;

101.play: Which I could with a ready guess declare,

1.play: Enter KING HENRY V, GLOUCESTER, BEDFORD, EXETER, WARWICK, WESTMORELAND, and Attendants

2.play: KING HENRY V: Where is my gracious Lord of Canterbury?

3.play: EXETER: Not here in presence.

4.play: KING HENRY V: Send for him, good uncle.

5.play: WESTMORELAND: Shall we call in the ambassador, my liege?

6.play: KING HENRY V: Not yet, my cousin: we would be resolved,

7.play: Before we hear him, of some things of weight

8.play: That task our thoughts, concerning us and France.

9.play: CANTERBURY: God and his angels guard your sacred throne

10.play: And make you long become it!

11.play: KING HENRY V: Sure, we thank you.

12.play: My learned lord, we pray you to proceed

13.play: And justly and religiously unfold

14.play: Why the law Salique that they have in France

15.play: Or should, or should not, bar us in our claim:

16.play: And God forbid, my dear and faithful lord,

17.play: That you should fashion, wrest, or bow your reading,

18.play: Or nicely charge your understanding soul

19.play: With opening titles miscreate, whose right

20.play: Suits not in native colours with the truth;

21.play: For God doth know how many now in health

22.play: Shall drop their blood in approbation

23.play: Of what your reverence shall incite us to.

24.play: Therefore take heed how you impawn our person,

25.play: How you awake our sleeping sword of war:

26.play: We charge you, in the name of God, take heed;

27.play: For never two such kingdoms did contend

28.play: Without much fall of blood; whose guiltless drops

29.play: Are every one a woe, a sore complaint

30.play: 'Gainst him whose wrong gives edge unto the swords

31.play: That make such waste in brief mortality.

32.play: Under this conjuration, speak, my lord;

33.play: For we will hear, note and believe in heart

34.play: That what you speak is in your conscience wash'd

35.play: As pure as sin with baptism.

36.play: CANTERBURY: Then hear me, gracious sovereign, and you peers,

37.play: That owe yourselves, your lives and services

38.play: To this imperial throne. There is no bar

39.play: To make against your highness' claim to France

40.play: But this, which they produce from Pharamond,

42.play: 'No woman shall succeed in Salique land:'

43.play: Which Salique land the French unjustly gloze

44.play: To be the realm of France, and Pharamond

45.play: The founder of this law and female bar.

46.play: Yet their own authors faithfully affirm

47.play: That the land Salique is in Germany,

48.play: Between the floods of Sala and of Elbe;

49.play: Where Charles the Great, having subdued the Saxons,

50.play: There left behind and settled certain French;

51.play: Who, holding in disdain the German women

52.play: For some dishonest manners of their life,

53.play: Establish'd then this law; to wit, no female

54.play: Should be inheritrix in Salique land:

55.play: Which Salique, as I said, 'twixt Elbe and Sala,

56.play: Is at this day in Germany call'd Meisen.

57.play: Then doth it well appear that Salique law

58.play: Was not devised for the realm of France:

59.play: Nor did the French possess the Salique land

60.play: Until four hundred one and twenty years

62.play: Idly supposed the founder of this law;

63.play: Who died within the year of our redemption

64.play: Four hundred twenty-six; and Charles the Great

65.play: Subdued the Saxons, and did seat the French

66.play: Beyond the river Sala, in the year

67.play: Eight hundred five. Besides, their writers say,

69.play: Did, as heir general, being descended

70.play: Of Blithild, which was daughter to King Clothair,

71.play: Make claim and title to the crown of France.

72.play: Hugh Capet also, who usurped the crown

73.play: Of Charles the duke of Lorraine, sole heir male

74.play: Of the true line and stock of Charles the Great,

75.play: To find his title with some shows of truth,

76.play: 'Through, in pure truth, it was corrupt and naught,

77.play: Convey'd himself as heir to the Lady Lingare,

78.play: Daughter to Charlemain, who was the son

79.play: To Lewis the emperor, and Lewis the son

80.play: Of Charles the Great. Also King Lewis the Tenth,

81.play: Who was sole heir to the usurper Capet,

82.play: Could not keep quiet in his conscience,

83.play: Wearing the crown of France, till satisfied

84.play: That fair Queen Isabel, his grandmother,

85.play: Was lineal of the Lady Ermengare,

86.play: Daughter to Charles the foresaid duke of Lorraine:

87.play: By the which marriage the line of Charles the Great

88.play: Was re-united to the crown of France.

89.play: So that, as clear as is the summer's sun.

90.play: King Pepin's title and Hugh Capet's claim,

91.play: King Lewis his satisfaction, all appear

92.play: To hold in right and title of the female:

93.play: So do the kings of France unto this day;

94.play: Howbeit they would hold up this Salique law

95.play: To bar your highness claiming from the female,

96.play: And rather choose to hide them in a net

97.play: Than amply to imbar their crooked titles

98.play: Usurp'd from you and your progenitors.

99.play: KING HENRY V: May I with right and conscience make this claim?

100.play: CANTERBURY: The sin upon my head, dread sovereign!

101.play: For in the book of Numbers is it writ,

102.play: When the man dies, let the inheritance

103.play: Descend unto the daughter. Gracious lord,

104.play: Stand for your own; unwind your bloody flag;

105.play: Look back into your mighty ancestors:

106.play: Go, my dread lord, to your great-grandsire's tomb,

107.play: From whom you claim; invoke his warlike spirit,

108.play: And your great-uncle's, Edward the Black Prince,

109.play: Who on the French ground play'd a tragedy,

110.play: Making defeat on the full power of France,

111.play: Whiles his most mighty father on a hill

112.play: Stood smiling to behold his lion's whelp

113.play: Forage in blood of French nobility.

114.play: O noble English. that could entertain

115.play: With half their forces the full Pride of France

116.play: And let another half stand laughing by,

117.play: All out of work and cold for action!

118.play: ELY: Awake remembrance of these valiant dead

119.play: And with your puissant arm renew their feats:

120.play: You are their heir; you sit upon their throne;

121.play: The blood and courage that renowned them

122.play: Runs in your veins; and my thrice-puissant liege

123.play: Is in the very May-morn of his youth,

124.play: Ripe for exploits and mighty enterprises.

125.play: EXETER: Your brother kings and monarchs of the earth

126.play: Do all expect that you should rouse yourself,

127.play: As did the former lions of your blood.

128.play: WESTMORELAND: They know your grace hath cause and means and might;

129.play: So hath your highness; never king of England

130.play: Had nobles richer and more loyal subjects,

131.play: Whose hearts have left their bodies here in England

132.play: And lie pavilion'd in the fields of France.

133.play: CANTERBURY: O, let their bodies follow, my dear liege,

134.play: With blood and sword and fire to win your right;

135.play: In aid whereof we of the spiritualty

136.play: Will raise your highness such a mighty sum

137.play: As never did the clergy at one time

138.play: Bring in to any of your ancestors.

139.play: KING HENRY V: We must not only arm to invade the French,

140.play: But lay down our proportions to defend

141.play: Against the Scot, who will make road upon us

143.play: CANTERBURY: They of those marches, gracious sovereign,

144.play: Shall be a wall sufficient to defend

145.play: Our inland from the pilfering borderers.

146.play: KING HENRY V: We do not mean the coursing snatchers only,

147.play: But fear the main intendment of the Scot,

148.play: Who hath been still a giddy neighbour to us;

149.play: For you shall read that my great-grandfather

150.play: Never went with his forces into France

151.play: But that the Scot on his unfurnish'd kingdom

152.play: Came pouring, like the tide into a breach,

153.play: With ample and brim fulness of his force,

154.play: Galling the gleaned land with hot assays,

155.play: Girding with grievous siege castles and towns;

156.play: That England, being empty of defence,

157.play: Hath shook and trembled at the ill neighbourhood.

158.play: CANTERBURY: She hath been then more fear'd than harm'd, my liege;

159.play: For hear her but exampled by herself:

160.play: When all her chivalry hath been in France

161.play: And she a mourning widow of her nobles,

162.play: She hath herself not only well defended

163.play: But taken and impounded as a stray

164.play: The King of Scots; whom she did send to France,

165.play: To fill King Edward's fame with prisoner kings

166.play: And make her chronicle as rich with praise

167.play: As is the ooze and bottom of the sea

168.play: With sunken wreck and sunless treasuries.

169.play: WESTMORELAND: But there's a saying very old and true,

172.play: For once the eagle England being in prey,

173.play: To her unguarded nest the weasel Scot

174.play: Comes sneaking and so sucks her princely eggs,

175.play: Playing the mouse in absence of the cat,

176.play: To tear and havoc more than she can eat.

177.play: EXETER: It follows then the cat must stay at home:

178.play: Yet that is but a crush'd necessity,

179.play: Since we have locks to safeguard necessaries,

180.play: And pretty traps to catch the petty thieves.

181.play: While that the armed hand doth fight abroad,

182.play: The advised head defends itself at home;

183.play: For government, though high and low and lower,

184.play: Put into parts, doth keep in one consent,

185.play: Congreeing in a full and natural close,

188.play: The state of man in divers functions,

190.play: To which is fixed, as an aim or butt,

191.play: Obedience: for so work the honey-bees,

192.play: Creatures that by a rule in nature teach

193.play: The act of order to a peopled kingdom.

194.play: They have a king and officers of sorts;

195.play: Where some, like magistrates, correct at home,

196.play: Others, like merchants, venture trade abroad,

197.play: Others, like soldiers, armed in their stings,

198.play: Make boot upon the summer's velvet buds,

199.play: Which pillage they with merry march bring home

200.play: To the tent-royal of their emperor;

201.play: Who, busied in his majesty, surveys

202.play: The singing masons building roofs of gold,

203.play: The civil citizens kneading up the honey,

204.play: The poor mechanic porters crowding in

205.play: Their heavy burdens at his narrow gate,

206.play: The sad-eyed justice, with his surly hum,

208.play: The lazy yawning drone. I this infer,

209.play: That many things, having full reference

210.play: To one consent, may work contrariously:

211.play: As many arrows, loosed several ways,

212.play: Come to one mark; as many ways meet in one town;

213.play: As many fresh streams meet in one salt sea;

214.play: As many lines close in the dial's centre;

215.play: So may a thousand actions, once afoot.

216.play: End in one purpose, and be all well borne

217.play: Without defeat. Therefore to France, my liege.

218.play: Divide your happy England into four;

219.play: Whereof take you one quarter into France,

220.play: And you withal shall make all Gallia shake.

221.play: If we, with thrice such powers left at home,

222.play: Cannot defend our own doors from the dog,

223.play: Let us be worried and our nation lose

224.play: The name of hardiness and policy.

225.play: KING HENRY V: Call in the messengers sent from the Dauphin.

226.play: Now are we well resolved; and, by God's help,

227.play: And yours, the noble sinews of our power,

228.play: France being ours, we'll bend it to our awe,

229.play: Or break it all to pieces: or there we'll sit,

230.play: Ruling in large and ample empery

231.play: O'er France and all her almost kingly dukedoms,

232.play: Or lay these bones in an unworthy urn,

233.play: Tombless, with no remembrance over them:

234.play: Either our history shall with full mouth

235.play: Speak freely of our acts, or else our grave,

236.play: Like Turkish mute, shall have a tongueless mouth,

237.play: Not worshipp'd with a waxen epitaph.

238.play: Now are we well prepared to know the pleasure

239.play: Of our fair cousin Dauphin; for we hear

240.play: Your greeting is from him, not from the king.

241.play: We are no tyrant, but a Christian king;

242.play: Unto whose grace our passion is as subject

243.play: As are our wretches fetter'd in our prisons:

244.play: Therefore with frank and with uncurbed plainness

248.play: KING HENRY V: We are glad the Dauphin is so pleasant with us;

249.play: His present and your pains we thank you for:

250.play: When we have march'd our rackets to these balls,

251.play: We will, in France, by God's grace, play a set

252.play: Shall strike his father's crown into the hazard.

253.play: Tell him he hath made a match with such a wrangler

254.play: That all the courts of France will be disturb'd

255.play: With chaces. And we understand him well,

256.play: How he comes o'er us with our wilder days,

257.play: Not measuring what use we made of them.

259.play: And therefore, living hence, did give ourself

260.play: To barbarous licence; as 'tis ever common

261.play: That men are merriest when they are from home.

262.play: But tell the Dauphin I will keep my state,

263.play: Be like a king and show my sail of greatness

264.play: When I do rouse me in my throne of France:

265.play: For that I have laid by my majesty

266.play: And plodded like a man for working-days,

267.play: But I will rise there with so full a glory

270.play: And tell the pleasant prince this mock of his

278.play: But this lies all within the will of God,

279.play: To whom I do appeal; and in whose name

280.play: Tell you the Dauphin I am coming on,

281.play: To venge me as I may and to put forth

282.play: My rightful hand in a well-hallow'd cause.

283.play: So get you hence in peace; and tell the Dauphin

284.play: His jest will savour but of shallow wit,

285.play: When thousands weep more than did laugh at it.

286.play: Convey them with safe conduct. Fare you well.

287.play: EXETER: This was a merry message.

288.play: KING HENRY V: We hope to make the sender blush at it.

289.play: Therefore, my lords, omit no happy hour

290.play: That may give furtherance to our expedition;

291.play: For we have now no thought in us but France,

292.play: Save those to God, that run before our business.

293.play: Therefore let our proportions for these wars

294.play: Be soon collected and all things thought upon

295.play: That may with reasonable swiftness add

296.play: More feathers to our wings; for, God before,
